title: 第 12 節
en: III. In the Taittiriya-upanishad, at the end of the second chapter, called the Brahmanandavalli, and again at the end of the tenth chapter, the text itself says : Ity upanishad, 'this is the Upanishad, the true doctrine.'
IV. In the Kaushitaki-upanishad II, I ; 2, we read : ' Let him not beg, this is the Upanishad for him who knows this.' Here upanishad stands for vrata or rahasya-vrata, rule.
Works on the Upanishads.
Anquetil Duperron, Oupnek'hat, 1801, 1802. See page clii.

Rammohun Roy, Translation of Several Principal Books, Passages, and Texts of the Veds. Second edition. London, Translation of the Moonduk-Oopnnishud of the Uthurvu Ved, p. 23. Translation of the Cena Upanishad, one of the Chapters of the Sanaa Veda, p. 41.
Translation of the Kut'h-Oopunishud of the Ujoor-Ved, p. 55.
Translation of the Ishopanishad, one of the Chapters of the Yajur Veda, p. 81.
H. T. Colebrooke, Miscellaneous Essays, in three volumes, K. J. H. Windischmann, Die Philosophic im Fortgange der Weltgeschichte, 1827-34.
F. W. Windischmann, Sancara, seu de theologumenis Vedanticorum, 1833.
E. Roer, The Taittiriya, Aitareya, *Sveta\yvatara, Kena Ij-a, Ka///a, Pra.ma, Mwidaka., and Mandukya. Upanishads translated; Bibliotheca Indica. Calcutta, 1853.
Rajendralal Mitra, The A7/andogya Upanishad, with extracts from the commentary of Sahkara ; Bibliotheca Indica. Calcutta, 1862.
INTRODUCTION. 1x3 XXXV E. B. Cowell, The Kaushitaki - brahma;/a - upanishad, edited with an English translation ; Bibliotheca Indica. Calcutta, 1861.
E. B. Cowell, The Maitri Upanishad, edited with an English translation; Bibliotheca Indica. Calcutta, 1870.
A. Weber, Die Va^rasu^i des Aj-vaghosha. Berlin, i860.
A. Weber, Die Rama-tapaniya Upanishad. Berlin, 1864.
A. Weber, Analyse der in Anquetil du Perron's Ubersetzung enthalten Upanishad ; Indische Studien, vol. i, p. 247 et seq.
A. E. Gough, The Philosophy of the Upanishads ; Calcutta Review, CXXXI.
P. Regnaud, Materiaux pour servir a l'histoire de la Philosophic de l'lnde. Paris, 1876.

Editions of the Upanishads, their commentaries and glosses have been published in the Tattvabodhini patrika, and by Poley (who has also translated several Upanishads into French), by Roer, Cowell, Rajendralal Mitra, Hara£andra Vidyabhushawa, Vuvanatha vSastri, Ramamaya Tarkaratna, and others. For fuller titles see Gildemeister, Bibliotheca Sanscrita, and E. Haas, Catalogue of Sanskrit and Pali Books in the British Museum, s. v. Upanishads.
lxxxvi UPANISHADS.

The -Oandogya-upanishad belongs to the Sama-veda. Together with the Br/had-ara«yaka, which belongs to the Ya^-ur-veda, it has contributed the most important materials to what may be called the orthodox philosophy of India, the Vedanta1, i.e. the end, the purpose, the highest object of the Veda. It consists of eight adhyayas or lectures, and formed part of a .Oandogya-brahmawa, in which it was preceded by two other adhyayas. While MSS. of the .AT/zandogya-upanishad and its commentary are frequent, no MSS. of the whole Brahmawa has been met with in Europe. Several scholars had actually doubted its existence, but Rajendralal Mitra2, in the Introduction to his translation of the isf/^andogya-upanishad, states that in India ' MSS. of the work are easily available, though as yet he has seen no commentary attached to the Brahmawa portion of any one of them.' ' According to general accep- 1 Vedanta, as a technical term, did not mean originally the last portions of the Veda, or chapters placed, as it were, at the end of a volume of Vedic literature, but the end, i. e. the object, the highest purpose of the Veda. There are, of course, passages, like the one in the Taittiriya-ara/zyaka (ed. Rajendralal Mitra, p. 820), which have been misunderstood both by native and European scholars, and where vedanta means simply the end of the Veda : — yo vedadau svaraft prokto vedante ka. pratish/AitaA, ' the Om which is pronounced at the beginning of the Veda, and has its place also at the end of the Veda.' Here vedanta stands simply in opposition to vedadau, and it is impossible to translate it, as Sayawa does, by Vedanta or Upanishad. Vedanta, in the sense of philosophy,occurs in the Taittiriya-ara/jyakafp. 817), in a verseof the Narayamyaupanishad, repeated in the Mu«</aka-upanishad III, 2, 6, and elsewhere, vedantavi^nanasunis&itarthaA, ' those who have well understood the object of the knowledge arising from the Vedanta,' not ' from the last books of the Veda ;' and -Svetasvatara-up. VI, 22, vedante paramaw guhyam, ' the highest mystery in the Vedanta.' Afterwards it is used in the plural also, e. g. Kshurikopanishad, 10 (Bibl. Ind. p. 210), purcrfariketi vedanteshu nigadyate, 'it is called purcrfarika in the Vedantas,' i. e. in the .Oandogya and other Upanishads, as the commentator says, but not in the last books of each Veda. A curious passage is found in the Gautama-sutras XIX, 12, where a distinction seems to be made between Upanishad and Vedanta. Sacred Books, vol. ii, p. 272.
2 .Oandogya-upanishad, translated by Rajendralal Mitra, Calcutta, 1862, Introduction, p. 17.

INTRODUCTION. lxxxvii tation,' he adds, ' the work embraces ten chapters, of which the first two are reckoned to be the Brahmaz/a, and the rest is known under the name of AV/andogya-upanishad. In their arrangement and style the two portions differ greatly, and judged by them they appear to be productions of very different ages, though both are evidently relics of pretty remote antiquity. Of the two chapters of the AV/andogyabrahmawa1, the first includes eight suktas (hymns) on the ceremony of marriage, and the rites necessary to be observed at the birth of a child. The first sukta is intended to be recited when offering an oblation to Agni on the occasion of a marriage, and its object is to pray for prosperity in behalf of the married couple. The second prays for long life, kind relatives, and a numerous progeny. The third is the marriage pledge by which the contracting parties bind themselves to each other. Its spirit may be guessed from a single verse. In talking of the unanimity with which they will dwell, the bridegroom addresses his bride, " That heart of thine shall be mine, and this heart of mine shall be thine2." The fourth and the fifth invoke Agni, Vayu, ATandramas, and Surya to bless the couple and ensure healthful progeny. The sixth is a mantra for offering an oblation on the birth of a child ; and the seventh and the eighth are prayers for its being healthy, wealthy, and powerful, not weak, poor, or mute, and to ensure a profusion of wealth and milch-cows. The first sukta of the second chapter is addressed to the Earth, Agni, and Indra, with a prayer for wealth, health, and prosperity ; the second, third, fourth, fifth, and sixth are mantras for offering oblations to cattle, the manes, Surya, and divers minor deities. The seventh is a curse upon worms, insects, flies, and other nuisances, and the last, the concluding mantra of the marriage ceremony, in which a general blessing is invoked for all concerned.'
After this statement there can be but little doubt that 1 It begins, Om, deva savita/z, pra suva yag-nam pra suva ya°Tiapatim bhagaya. The second begins, yah pra&ya/w disi sarpara§a esha te baliA.
2 Yad etad dhr/dayam tava tad astu hrz'dayam mama, Yad idawz hn'dayam mama tad astu hn'dayam tava.
lxxxviii UPANISHADS.

this Upanishad originally formed part of a Brahma;/a. This may have been called either by a general name, the Brahmawa of the A7zandogas, the followers of the Sama-veda, or, on account of the prominent place occupied in it by the Upanishad, the Upanishad-brahmawa *. In that case it would be one of the eight Brahmawas of the Sama-veda, enumerated by Kumarila Bha//a and others2, and called simply Upanishad, scil. Brahmawa.

The text of the Upanishad with the commentary of Sankara and the gloss of Anandagiri has been published in the Bibliotheca Indica. The edition can only claim the character of a manuscript, and of a manuscript not always very correctly read.
A translation of the Upanishad was published, likewise in the Bibliotheca Indica, by Rajendralal Mitra.

It is one of the Upanishads that was translated into Persian under the auspices of Dara Shukoh3, and from Persian into French by Anquetil Duperron, in his Oupnekhat, i.e. Secretum Tegendum. Portions of it were translated into English by Colebrooke in his Miscellaneous Essays, into Latin and German by F. W. Windischmann, in his Sankara, seu de theologumenis Vedanticorum (Bonn, 1833), and in a work published by his father, K. J. H. Windischmann, Die Philosophic im Fortgang der Weltgeschichte (Bonn, 1827-34). Professor A. Weber has treated of this Upanishad in his Indische Studien I, 254 ; likewise M. P. Regnaud in his Materiaux pour servir a l'histoire de la philosophic de Tlnde (Paris, 1876) and Mr. Gough in several articles on 'the Philosophy of the Upanishads,' in the Calcutta Review, No. CXXXI.
zh: III. 在《泰帝利亞奧義書》（Taittiriya-upanishad）中，於第二章末尾（稱為《梵樂喜篇》Brahmanandavalli）以及第十章末尾，經文本身云：Ity upanishad，「此乃奧義書，真實之教義」。
四、在《考希塔基奧義書》（Kaushitaki-upanishad）第二卷第一章第二節中，我們讀到：「他不應乞討，這是知道此事者的奧義」（'Let him not beg, this is the Upanishad for him who knows this.'）。此處奧義（upanishad）代表誓願或祕密誓願（vrata or rahasya-vrata），即規則（rule）之意。
奧義書著作。
安凱蒂爾‧杜佩龍，《奧義書集》（Oupnek'hat），1801, 1802 年。見第 clii 頁。
拉莫罕‧羅易，《吠陀之諸要書、片段及經文譯叢》第二版。倫敦，《烏陀吠陀之蒙德克‧烏帕尼沙德譯文》第23頁。《賽那奧義書譯文》，乃《賽那吠陀》之諸篇章之一，第41頁。
《烏朱爾吠陀》之《庫特奧義書》譯文，第五十五頁。
《伊莎奧義書》譯文，取自《夜柔吠陀》之一章，第81頁。
H. T. 考爾布魯克《雜文集》，共三卷；K. J. H. 溫迪施曼《世界史進程中的哲學》，一八二七─三四年。
弗‧威‧溫迪施曼恩，《商羯羅：或吠檀多學派的神學論述》，1833年。

E. Roer，《泰提利耶奧義書》、《愛達囉耶奧義書》、《白馬奧義書》（*Śvetāśvatara*）、《何那奧義書》（Kena Upaniṣad）、《迦多奧義書》（Kaṭha Upaniṣad）、《普拉什那奧義書》（Praśna Upaniṣad）、《蒙達卡奧義書》（Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad）與《蛙奧義書》（Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad）譯本，《印度叢書》叢刊，加爾各答，1853 年。
拉吉恩德拉拉爾‧米特拉，《雅姜婆羅多奧義書》（Aitareya Upanishad），附尚卡拉注疏摘錄；《印度文庫》叢書，加爾各答，1862年。
導論。1x3 XXXV E. B. Cowell，《考希塔基‧婆羅門奧義書》(The Kaushitaki-brahma-upanishad)，英譯編訂本；《印度文庫》(Bibliotheca Indica)，加爾各答，1861年。
E. B. Cowell, 《迦多羅奧義書》(The Maitri Upanishad)，英譯並編註；《印度叢書》(Bibliotheca Indica)，加爾各答，1870 年。
A. Weber，《馬鳴的婆羅素篇》（Die Varasu^i des Aj-vaghosha），柏林，1860年。
A. Weber, 《羅摩塔帕尼亞奧義書》(Die Rama-tapaniya Upanishad)。柏林，1864。
A. Weber，《安克蒂爾‧杜‧佩龍譯本所含奧義書之分析》；《印度研究》第一卷，第247頁及後續頁碼。
A. E.高夫，《奧義書的哲學》；《加爾各答評論》，第一百三十一卷。
P. Regnaud, 《印度哲學史資料彙編》，巴黎，1876年。

關於《奧義書》的版本及其註疏和詞彙編已在《真理知識雜誌》（Tattvabodhini patrika）中刊行，並由波利（Poley）、羅爾（Roer）、考威爾（Cowell）、拉傑德拉拉爾‧米特拉（Rajendralal Mitra）、哈拉詹德拉‧維迪亞布沙瓦（Harañandra Vidyabhushawa）、武瓦那塔‧沙斯特里（Vuvanatha Srastri）、拉瑪亞亞‧塔卡拉特納（Ramamaya Tarkaratna）及他人出版。波利並將多部《奧義書》譯為法文。如需完整書名，詳見吉爾德邁斯特（Gildemeister）的《梵語文庫》（Bibliotheca Sanscrita）及哈斯（E. Haas）的《大英博物館梵語及巴利語書籍目錄》（Catalogue of Sanskrit and Pali Books in the British Museum）中關於《奧義書》（s. v. Upanishads）的條目。
lxxxvi 奧義書。

《香德歌亞奧義書》（Oandogya-upanishad）屬於《沙摩吠陀》（Sama-veda）。它與屬於《夜柔吠陀》（Ya^-ur-veda）的《布利哈達蘭亞卡奧義書》（Br/had-ara«yaka）一起，為印度的正統哲學（即《吠檀多》）提供了最重要的資料。《吠檀多》（Vedanta）的意思是吠陀的終極、目的、最高對象。該奧義書由八個阿笈多（adhyayas，講章）組成，曾是《香德歌亞梵書》（.Oandogya-brahmawa）的一部分，在梵書中它之前還有另外兩個阿笈多。儘管《香德歌亞奧義書》及其註釋本的抄本在歐洲很常見，但整部梵書的抄本卻未曾在歐洲發現過。許多學者實際上曾懷疑其存在，但拉吉恩德拉拉爾‧米特拉1（Rajendralal Mitra）在其《香德歌亞奧義書》譯本的導論中指出，在印度「該著作的抄本容易獲得，儘管他至今還未見到任何梵書部分附有註釋的抄本」。 據普遍認可2，《吠檀多》作為專業術語，最初並非指吠陀的最後部分或位於吠陀文獻卷冊末尾的章節，而是指終極，即吠陀的對象、最高目的。當然存在著某些段落，如《泰帝利耶阿蘭亞卡》（Taittiriya-ara/zyaka，米特拉版，第820頁）中的段落，它們已遭到本地和歐洲學者的誤解，其中vedanta僅表示吠陀的終結：——「yo vedadau svaraft prokto vedante ka. pratish/AitaA」，即「在吠陀開篇誦念的唵（Om），也在吠陀末尾有其位置」。在此vedanta純粹與vedadau相對立，不可能如沙耶納（Sayawa）所做的那樣，將其譯為《吠檀多》或《奧義書》。在哲學意義上的《吠檀多》出現在《泰帝利耶阿蘭亞卡》（第817頁）中，出現在《那羅延亞奧義書》（Narayamyaupanishad）的一首詩句中，該詩句亦見於《蒙杜卡奧義書》（Mu«</aka-upanishad）第三卷第2節第6經文及其他處，即「vedantavi^nanasunis&itarthaA」，意為「那些充分領悟源自吠檀多知識之對象者」，而非「源自吠陀最後各書者」；以及《白色雪特瓦塔拉奧義書》（-Svetasvatara-up.）第六卷第22經，「vedante paramaw guhyam」，即「吠檀多中的最高奧秘」。後來它也用於複數形式，例如《斧形奧義書》（Kshurikopanishad）第10經（印度文庫版，第210頁），「purcrfariketi vedanteshu nigadyate」，意為「在吠檀多中稱為purcrfarika」，即如註釋家所言，在《香德歌亞奧義書》及其他奧義書中稱此，而非在各吠陀的最後各書中。在《高坦摩經》（Gautama-sutras）第十九卷第12經中可見一個有趣的段落，似乎在奧義書與吠檀多之間作出了區分。見《聖書》第二卷，第272頁。
2. Oandogya-upanishad，拉堅德拉‧勒‧米特拉譯，加爾各答，1862年，導論，第17頁。

導論。lxxxvii 他補充道：「這部著作包括十章，其中前兩章被認為是婆羅門書（Brahmana），其餘部分則以《愛多葉亞奧義書》（AV/andogya-upanishad）之名著稱。在編排與文體上，這兩個部分相差極大，據此判斷，它們似乎出自非常不同的時代，儘管兩者顯然都是相當遠古的遺物。在《愛多葉亞婆羅門書》（AV/andogya-brahmana）的兩章中，第一章包含八首贊歌（hymns）論述婚禮儀式，以及嬰兒誕生時必須遵守的儀軌。第一首贊歌擬在婚禮場合向火神阿耆尼（Agni）獻祭時誦讀，其目的是為已婚夫婦祈禱幸福昌盛。第二首為長壽、親族仁慈與眾多後嗣而祈禱。第三首是婚約，訂約雙方以此相互約束。其精神可由一句經文窺知。新郎在談及他們將如何一心同意地共同生活時，向新娘說道：「那汝心將為我心，此我心將為汝心。」第四首與第五首祈求火神阿耆尼（Agni）、風神伐由（Vayu）、月神月天（Andrama）與日神蘇利耶（Surya）祝福婚偶並確保子嗣強健。第六首是嬰兒誕生時獻祭的咒語（mantra）；第七與第八首為祈禱其健康、富足與強大，而非虛弱、貧窮或啞口無言，並祈求財富與奶牛繁茂。第二章的第一首贊歌祭祀於地、火神阿耆尼（Agni）與因陀羅（Indra），祈求財富、健康與繁榮；第二、三、四、五、六首則為祭祀家畜、祖靈、日神蘇利耶（Surya）與各種次要神祇的獻祭咒語（mantras）。第七首是對蟲蛆、昆蟲、蒼蠅與其他害蟲的詛咒，最後一首則是婚禮儀式的結束咒語，在其中為所有相關人等祈求普遍的祝福。」
在此陳述之後，幾乎不容有任何疑問，其開首為「Om, deva savitá, pra suva yag-nam pra suva yajñapatim bhagaya」。第二首開首為「yah prajayah disi sparparsha esha te balih」。
願你心中所珍愛者亦為我心所珍愛，願我心中所珍愛者亦為你心所珍愛。
lxxxviii 奧義書。

這部奧義書原本是某個梵書（Brahmawa）的一部分。它可能被稱為閻摩陀提婆經派追隨者（即誦讚吠陀信徒）的一般名稱《閻摩陀提婆經梵書》（Brahmawa of the Aṭlandogas），或者由於奧義書在其中占有突出地位，而被稱為《奧義書梵書》（Upanishad-brahmawa）*。在這種情況下，它將是库玛里拉·跋陀（Kumarila Bha//a）等人所列舉的《誦讚吠陀》的八部梵書之一，簡稱為《奧義書》（Upanishad），即梵書（scil. Brahmawa）。
《奧義書》的文本連同商羯羅（Sankara）的評注與阿難陀吉里（Anandagiri）的註疏已由《印度文庫》出版。該版本僅具有手稿的性質，且是一份並非總是被正確解讀的手稿。
拉真德拉拉爾‧彌特拉（Rajendralal Mitra）在《印度文庫》中亦發表了奧義書的譯本。

這是奧義書之一，曾在達拉‧舒科（Dara Shukoh）的支持下被譯成波斯文，再由安凱蒂爾‧迪佩隆（Anquetil Duperron）從波斯文譯成法文，收入其《烏普尼克哈特》（Oupnekhat，即 Secretum Tegendum）一書中。其片段內容曾被柯爾布魯克（Colebrooke）譯成英文，收於其《雜文集》（Miscellaneous Essays）；被 F. W. 溫迪施曼（F. W. Windischmann）譯成拉丁文和德文，收於其《商羯羅，或吠檀多主義的神學學說》（Sankara, seu de theologumenis Vedanticorum）一書（波恩，1833 年）；以及其父 K. J. H. 溫迪施曼（K. J. H. Windischmann）所著《世界歷史進程中的哲學》（Die Philosophic im Fortgang der Weltgeschichte）一書（波恩，1827-34 年）。韋伯教授（Professor A. Weber）在其《印度研究》（Indische Studien）第一卷第 254 頁中討論過此奧義書；同樣地，雷格諾（M. P. Regnaud）在其《印度哲學史料匯編》（Materiaux pour servir a l'histoire de la philosophic de l'Inde）一書（巴黎，1876 年）中也有論述；高夫先生（Mr. Gough）亦在《加爾各答評論》（Calcutta Review）第 CXXXI 期的數篇文章中論及《奧義書哲學》（'the Philosophy of the Upanishads'）。
